topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt finality of a closing door. Professor Albright was leaving. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I reread the subject line three times, the words mocking my inability to process the shift in reality. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent lights of my cramped dorm room suddenly buzzing with an aggressive intensity. Every word of his guidance, every gentle correction, suddenly felt like a fragile scaffolding collapsing around my entire project. I pressed my palms flat against the desk, the wood cool and unforgiving beneath my skin. A tremor started in my hands, making the keyboard dance erratically. *No. This can't be happening.* I slowly pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor, the sound echoing too loudly in the silence.

[story 2]

I found myself pacing the length of my apartment, the worn carpet muffling my frantic footsteps. The news about Dr. Vance’s departure had hit me like a physical blow. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, as I gripped the edge of the windowsill until my knuckles turned white. It wasn't just the loss of a supervisor; it felt like the sudden erasure of my entire academic trajectory. I stopped abruptly, leaning my forehead against the cool glass. My vision swam for a second, the edges blurring into an indistinct smear of panic. "This isn't right," I whispered, the sound raspy and thin. I started running a hand through my hair, pulling at the strands as if trying to physically pull the situation back into alignment, but the effort only seemed to deepen the sense of disorientation.

[story 3]

When the announcement finally filtered through the departmental mailing list, I physically recoiled, stumbling backward until my back hit the wall. My heart hammered against my ribs with a frantic, uneven rhythm, loud enough that I was certain someone could hear it. I couldn't look at the screen; the text seemed to swim before my eyes. A strange, heavy pressure settled in my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the image away, but the details—the departure date, the vacant chair—refused to dissipate. I opened my eyes, and the room seemed too bright, too sharp. I stood there, utterly still, a statue carved from sudden, overwhelming dread, unable to move or speak.
